explanation: OSPF is a Link State routing protocol. Each OSPF router floods LSAs (Link State Advertisements) to all routers in the area, builds a complete topology database (LSDB), and runs Dijkstra's SPF to compute routes.

q: OSPF uses which protocol directly over IP?
explanation: OSPF runs directly over IP using IP protocol number 89. It does not use TCP or UDP. OSPF uses its own reliability mechanisms for flooding LSAs and maintaining adjacencies via Hello packets.

q: In OSPF, what is the purpose of the Designated Router (DR)?
options: To perform all routing decisions for the area
To reduce the number of adjacencies and LSA flooding on broadcast networks
To redistribute routes from other protocols
explanation: On broadcast networks (e.g., Ethernet), OSPF elects a DR and BDR. All other routers (DROthers) form adjacencies only with the DR and BDR, reducing the number of adjacencies from O(nÂ²) to O(n) and minimizing LSA flooding.

q: In hierarchical routing, the Internet is divided into Autonomous Systems (AS). Routing within an AS is called:
explanation: Routing within an Autonomous System uses Interior Gateway Protocols (IGPs) such as OSPF or RIP. Routing between different Autonomous Systems uses Exterior Gateway Protocols (EGPs) such as BGP.

q: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is classified as which type of routing protocol?
explanation: BGP is a Path Vector protocol. Unlike distance vector, BGP advertises the complete AS path to each destination (e.g., AS1â†’AS2â†’AS3), allowing loop detection and policy-based routing decisions.

q: BGP uses which transport protocol and port number?
explanation: BGP uses TCP port 179 for establishing sessions between BGP peers. Using TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery of BGP messages (OPEN, UPDATE, NOTIFICATION, KEEPALIVE).

q: What is the primary purpose of ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)?
options: Resolve domain names to IP addresses
Map IP addresses to MAC addresses
Map MAC addresses to IP addresses
explanation: ARP resolves IPv4 addresses to MAC (hardware) addresses on a local network segment. When a host needs to send a packet to an IP address on the same subnet, it uses ARP to find the corresponding MAC address.

q: An ARP request is sent as a:
options: Unicast frame to the destination MAC
Multicast frame to a group MAC
Broadcast to all IP addresses on the subnet
explanation: An ARP request is broadcast at the Ethernet layer (destination MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) because the sender does not yet know the destination's MAC address. All hosts on the segment receive and process the ARP request.

q: An ARP reply is sent as a:
options: Unicast directly to the requesting host
explanation: After receiving an ARP request, the host with the matching IP address sends a unicast ARP reply directly back to the requester's MAC address (known from the ARP request). This avoids unnecessary traffic to all hosts.

q: ARP cache entries are stored temporarily to reduce ARP traffic. What happens to a cache entry if it is not refreshed?
options: It remains permanently until manually deleted
It expires after a timeout (typically 20 minutes on most OS)
It is broadcast to all hosts
It is moved to the routing table
explanation: ARP cache entries have a timeout (typically around 20 minutes on most operating systems). Expired entries are removed, causing a new ARP request to be sent when that IP is needed again. Dynamic entries age out automatically.

q: Which protocol is the reverse of ARP, used by diskless workstations to find their IP address from their MAC address?
explanation: RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) does the reverse of ARP: it maps a known MAC address to an IP address. Diskless workstations used RARP to discover their IP address at boot time. RARP has been superseded by BOOTP and DHCP.

q: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) operates at which layer of the TCP/IP model?
explanation: ICMP is a Network Layer protocol encapsulated directly within IP (IP protocol number 1). It is used to send error messages and operational information about IP network conditions, not for data transport.

q: The 'ping' command uses which ICMP message types?
options: ICMP Type 8 (Echo Request) and Type 0 (Echo Reply)
explanation: Ping sends ICMP Echo Request (Type 8) messages to the target host. The target responds with ICMP Echo Reply (Type 0) messages. Round-trip time is measured from this exchange to test reachability and latency.

q: The 'traceroute' command works by sending packets with progressively increasing:
options: TTL (Time To Live) values starting from 1
explanation: Traceroute sends packets with TTL=1 first. Each router decrements TTL; when TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) back to the source, revealing that router's identity. TTL is incremented for each subsequent probe.

q: Which ICMP message is returned to traceroute's source when a packet's TTL expires at an intermediate router?
explanation: When a router receives a packet with TTL=1 and decrements it to 0, it sends ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0: TTL expired in transit) back to the source. Traceroute uses these replies to map each hop along the path.

q: DHCP stands for:
explanation: DHCP stands for Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol. It automatically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters (subnet mask, default gateway, DNS server) to clients, eliminating manual configuration.

q: The DHCP DORA process stands for:
explanation: DHCP DORA: (1) Discover â€” client broadcasts to find DHCP servers; (2) Offer â€” server offers an IP address; (3) Request â€” client broadcasts requesting the offered IP; (4) Acknowledge â€” server confirms the assignment with lease parameters.

q: The DHCP Discover message is sent as a:
options: Broadcast (255.255.255.255) because the client has no IP yet
Multicast to the DHCP group address
Anycast to the nearest DHCP server
explanation: A DHCP client has no IP address when it starts, so it sends a DHCP Discover as a limited broadcast (source 0.0.0.0, destination 255.255.255.255) using UDP. All DHCP servers on the subnet receive this broadcast.

q: DHCP uses which UDP port numbers for client and server?
explanation: DHCP clients use UDP source port 68 (BOOTPC) and send to destination UDP port 67 (BOOTPS) on the server. Servers respond using source port 67 to destination port 68. These ports are inherited from BOOTP.

q: A DHCP lease defines:
options: The MAC address permanently assigned to a host
The period for which an IP address is assigned to a client
The routing table entry for the client
The DNS domain name for the client
explanation: A DHCP lease is the time duration for which a client is allowed to use an assigned IP address. Before the lease expires, the client must renew it (using DHCP Request/Acknowledge). If not renewed, the IP returns to the available pool.

q: At what percentage of its lease time does a DHCP client first attempt to renew its lease?
explanation: A DHCP client attempts unicast renewal at 50% of the lease time (T1 timer). If no response, it broadcasts renewal at 87.5% (T2 timer). If still no response by lease expiration, the client must restart the DORA process.

q: NAT (Network Address Translation) primarily solves which problem?
options: Security through encryption of IP headers
IPv4 address exhaustion by sharing public IPs among many private hosts
Quality of Service for VoIP traffic
explanation: NAT allows multiple hosts with private IP addresses (RFC 1918: 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, 192.168.x.x) to share a single or small pool of public IP addresses, significantly extending the usable IPv4 address space.

q: In static NAT, the mapping between private and public IP addresses is:
options: Dynamic and changes with each connection
Determined automatically by the NAT device
explanation: Static NAT creates a permanent one-to-one mapping between a specific private IP address and a specific public IP address. It is used when a host (e.g., a web server) must always be reachable at the same public IP address.

q: In dynamic NAT, addresses are assigned from:
options: A single public IP with different port numbers
A pool of public IP addresses on a first-come, first-served basis
explanation: Dynamic NAT maps private IP addresses to public IPs from a configured pool. Assignments are temporary and made when needed. If the pool is exhausted, new connections are denied. It is still a one-to-one IP mapping (no port translation).

q: PAT (Port Address Translation), also called NAT Overload, differs from basic NAT in that it:
options: Uses a pool of public IPs without port modification
Maps many private IPs to a single public IP by also translating port numbers
Requires static mappings for each private host
explanation: PAT (also called NAPT or NAT Overload) maps multiple private IP:port combinations to a single public IP using different source port numbers. This allows thousands of internal hosts to share one public IP, making it the most common form of NAT used in home routers.

q: Which RFC address ranges are designated as private (non-routable on the public Internet)?
explanation: RFC 1918 defines three private address ranges: 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B, covers 172.16.x.xâ€“172.31.x.x), and 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C). These are not routed on the public Internet and require NAT for Internet access.

q: Which of the following is NOT a feature of OSPF compared to RIP?
options: Uses link cost metrics based on bandwidth
Uses hop count as the only metric
explanation: RIP uses hop count as its sole metric (max 15). OSPF uses cost based on link bandwidth (cost = 10^8/bandwidth). OSPF has no hop count limitation, converges faster, and supports hierarchical design with areas. Hop count as only metric is a RIP characteristic, not OSPF.

q: In OSPF, the backbone area to which all other areas must connect is called:
explanation: OSPF Area 0 is the backbone area. All other OSPF areas must connect to Area 0 either directly or via a virtual link. Inter-area routing information passes through the backbone, ensuring loop-free inter-area routing.

q: A router connecting two different OSPF areas (including the backbone) is called an:
explanation: An Area Border Router (ABR) is an OSPF router that has interfaces in multiple OSPF areas, including the backbone (Area 0). It maintains separate LSDBs for each area and summarizes routes between areas.

q: BGP route selection uses the AS_PATH attribute primarily for:
explanation: BGP's AS_PATH attribute lists all Autonomous Systems a route advertisement has traversed. If a router sees its own AS number in the AS_PATH, it discards the route (loop prevention). Shorter AS_PATH is also preferred in BGP's best-path selection process.

q: Gratuitous ARP is used for:
options: Resolving remote IP addresses across routers
Detecting IP address conflicts and updating other hosts' ARP caches when an IP changes
Communicating between routers in different VLANs
explanation: A Gratuitous ARP is an ARP request where the sender's IP and target IP are the same (the sender's own IP). It serves two purposes: (1) detect IP address conflicts (if someone replies, there's a duplicate), and (2) update ARP caches of neighboring hosts after an IP or MAC change.

q: Proxy ARP allows a router to:
options: Cache ARP replies from remote subnets
Reply to ARP requests on behalf of hosts on a different subnet
Forward ARP broadcasts across router interfaces
Assign IP addresses to clients requesting them via ARP
explanation: With Proxy ARP, a router answers ARP requests for IP addresses on a different subnet with its own MAC address, acting as a proxy. This allows hosts without a configured default gateway to still reach remote networks. It is generally discouraged in modern networks.

q: What is the total size of a UDP header?
explanation: A UDP header consists of 4 fields â€” Source Port (2B), Destination Port (2B), Length (2B), and Checksum (2B) â€” totaling 8 bytes.

q: Which field in the UDP header specifies the total length of the UDP datagram (header + data)?
explanation: The Length field in the UDP header holds the total size of the UDP datagram in bytes, including the 8-byte header and the data payload.

q: The checksum field in UDP is:
options: Optional in IPv4 but mandatory in IPv6
Mandatory in both IPv4 and IPv6
explanation: In IPv4, the UDP checksum is optional (a value of 0 means it is not computed). In IPv6, there is no header checksum, so the UDP checksum becomes mandatory to ensure end-to-end integrity.

q: UDP is described as a connectionless protocol because:
options: It uses IP addresses to route packets
It does not establish a connection before sending data
It requires acknowledgement for every packet
explanation: UDP does not perform any handshake or connection setup before transmitting data. Each datagram is sent independently, making it connectionless.

q: Which of the following applications typically uses UDP?
explanation: DNS uses UDP (port 53) for quick query-response lookups because the overhead of TCP connection setup is unnecessary for small, single-exchange messages.

q: VoIP applications prefer UDP over TCP primarily because:
options: Real-time audio tolerates some packet loss better than the delay caused by retransmission
explanation: VoIP requires low latency. TCP retransmissions introduce variable delay (jitter), which is worse for voice quality than occasional packet loss. UDP's no-retransmit model suits real-time streams.

q: Which protocol uses UDP on port 53?
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) primarily uses UDP on port 53 for standard queries and responses. TCP port 53 is used for zone transfers or responses exceeding 512 bytes.

q: Streaming video services (e.g., live broadcast) often use UDP because:
options: Lost frames can be skipped without stalling playback, prioritising continuity
UDP has higher throughput limits than TCP
explanation: For live video, a slightly degraded frame is preferable to waiting for retransmission. UDP allows continuous streaming without the head-of-line blocking that TCP retransmissions would cause.

q: What is the minimum value the UDP Length field can hold?
explanation: The UDP Length field includes the header itself (8 bytes). A datagram with no data payload still has a length of 8, which is the minimum valid value.

q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of UDP?
explanation: UDP does not guarantee in-order delivery. It is connectionless, unreliable (no ACKs/retransmission), and provides no flow or congestion control.

q: What is the minimum size of a TCP header (with no options)?
explanation: The TCP header without options is 20 bytes (5 Ã— 32-bit words). The Data Offset field specifies the header length in 32-bit words; its minimum value is 5.

q: Which TCP header field indicates the number of 32-bit words in the TCP header?
explanation: The Data Offset (also called Header Length) field is 4 bits and specifies the TCP header length in 32-bit words. A value of 5 means 20 bytes (no options).

q: The TCP Sequence Number field is used to:
options: Number each byte of data so the receiver can reassemble segments in order
explanation: The Sequence Number field holds the byte offset of the first data byte in this segment (or the ISN during SYN). It allows the receiver to reassemble out-of-order segments.

q: The TCP Acknowledgement Number field contains:
options: The sequence number of the last byte received
The next sequence number the receiver expects to receive
The size of the sender's buffer
The number of bytes already sent
explanation: The ACK number is a cumulative acknowledgement: it tells the sender the next byte number the receiver expects, implying all bytes up to ACK-1 have been received successfully.

q: Which TCP flag is set to initiate a connection?
explanation: The SYN (Synchronize) flag is set in the first segment of the TCP three-way handshake to initiate a connection and synchronise sequence numbers.

q: Which TCP flag is used to gracefully terminate a connection?
explanation: The FIN (Finish) flag signals that the sender has no more data to send and wishes to close its half of the TCP connection gracefully.

q: The TCP Window Size field in the header is used for:
options: Flow control â€” informing the sender how many bytes the receiver can accept
Setting the TTL of the segment
explanation: The Window Size (advertised window) field tells the sender the number of bytes the receiver's buffer can currently accept. This is the primary mechanism for TCP flow control.

q: During TCP's three-way handshake, what is the correct sequence of segments?
explanation: The three-way handshake proceeds: (1) Client sends SYN, (2) Server replies SYN-ACK (sets both SYN and ACK flags), (3) Client sends ACK. After step 3, the connection is established.

q: In the TCP three-way handshake, the SYN-ACK segment sent by the server:
options: Acknowledges the client's SYN and sends the server's own ISN
explanation: The SYN-ACK is a combined segment: the ACK flag acknowledges the client's ISN+1, and the SYN flag carries the server's own Initial Sequence Number (ISN) for the reverse direction.

q: After the TCP three-way handshake completes, how many ISNs have been exchanged?
explanation: Each side generates its own Initial Sequence Number (ISN). The client's ISN is sent in the SYN, and the server's ISN is sent in the SYN-ACK. Two ISNs are exchanged in total.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about TCP's three-way handshake?
options: The client sends data in the SYN segment
The connection is fully established after the SYN-ACK
The third segment (ACK) may carry data (piggybacking)
Four segments are exchanged during connection setup
explanation: The third ACK from the client may carry application data piggybacked on it. This is legal and common in practice (e.g., HTTP request in the third segment).

q: TCP connection termination using the four-way handshake involves:
options: FIN â†’ ACK â†’ FIN â†’ ACK
SYN â†’ FIN â†’ ACK â†’ RST
RST â†’ ACK â†’ FIN â†’ ACK
explanation: Four-way termination: (1) Active closer sends FIN, (2) Passive closer sends ACK, (3) Passive closer sends its own FIN, (4) Active closer sends final ACK. TCP is full-duplex, so each direction closes independently.

q: After sending the final ACK in TCP four-way termination, the active closer enters:
explanation: The active closer enters TIME_WAIT after sending the final ACK. It waits 2Ã—MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) to ensure the final ACK reached the passive closer and to let stale segments expire.

q: Why does TCP use a TIME_WAIT period of 2Ã—MSL?
options: To allow the server to reuse the port immediately
To ensure the final ACK is received and old duplicate segments expire from the network
explanation: 2Ã—MSL covers the round-trip time for the final ACK plus any delayed duplicates. If the final ACK is lost, the passive closer retransmits its FIN, and the active closer can re-send the ACK within TIME_WAIT.

q: The passive closer in TCP four-way termination enters which state after sending its FIN?
explanation: After the passive closer sends its own FIN (step 3), it enters LAST_ACK, waiting for the final ACK from the active closer. Once received, it transitions to CLOSED.

q: TCP flow control is implemented using:
options: The TTL field in the IP header
The advertised (receiver) window in the TCP header
explanation: The receiver advertises its available buffer space in the Window Size field of every ACK. The sender must not have more than this many unacknowledged bytes in flight at any time.

q: If the receiver's advertised window is 0, what does the sender do?
options: Stop sending data and periodically send 1-byte probe segments (persist timer)
explanation: When rwnd=0, the sender stops transmitting but starts a persist timer. It periodically sends 1-byte window probe segments to check if the receiver's window has opened, preventing deadlock.

q: The effective window (amount a sender can transmit) in TCP is:
explanation: The sender limits its outstanding data to min(cwnd, rwnd): cwnd is the congestion window (network capacity estimate) and rwnd is the receiver's advertised window (buffer capacity). The smaller constrains transmission.

q: In TCP slow start, the congestion window (cwnd) grows:
options: Exponentially â€” by 1 MSS for each ACK received (doubles per RTT)
explanation: During slow start, cwnd increases by 1 MSS for every ACK received. Since each RTT yields cwnd ACKs, cwnd doubles each RTT â€” exponential growth. This continues until cwnd reaches ssthresh.

q: TCP slow start phase ends when:
options: cwnd reaches or exceeds ssthresh (slow start threshold)
explanation: Slow start transitions to congestion avoidance when cwnd â‰¥ ssthresh. If a loss event occurs before this, ssthresh is updated and slow start may restart from cwnd=1.

q: In TCP congestion avoidance (AIMD), cwnd increases by approximately:
explanation: Congestion avoidance uses additive increase: cwnd grows by roughly MSSÃ—(MSS/cwnd) per ACK, totalling ~1 MSS per RTT. This is the 'AI' (Additive Increase) in AIMD.

q: In AIMD, when a timeout (loss) is detected, TCP:
options: Halves cwnd and continues congestion avoidance
Sets ssthresh = cwnd/2, resets cwnd = 1 MSS, and restarts slow start
explanation: On timeout (most severe loss signal): ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = 1 MSS, and the sender re-enters slow start. This is the 'MD' (Multiplicative Decrease) on timeout.

q: Fast retransmit is triggered when the sender receives:
explanation: TCP fast retransmit fires upon receiving 3 duplicate (identical) ACKs. Three dup-ACKs indicate a single segment was lost but subsequent segments are still reaching the receiver, so the network is not fully congested.

q: After fast retransmit (3 duplicate ACKs), TCP Reno sets ssthresh and cwnd to:
options: ssthresh = cwnd/2; cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS (fast recovery)
explanation: TCP Reno fast recovery: ssthresh = cwnd/2, then cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS (the 3 dup-ACKs imply 3 segments left the network). The sender retransmits the lost segment and enters fast recovery, not slow start.

q: During TCP fast recovery, cwnd is inflated by 1 MSS for each additional duplicate ACK received because:
options: Each dup-ACK signals another segment has left the network, temporarily increasing available window
It compensates for the dropped segment
The sender is in slow start
explanation: Each duplicate ACK in fast recovery means a new segment has been received (and hence another segment has exited the network). Inflating cwnd by 1 MSS per dup-ACK lets the sender keep new data flowing.

q: When a new (non-duplicate) ACK arrives during TCP fast recovery, the sender:
options: Sets cwnd = ssthresh and transitions to congestion avoidance
explanation: A new ACK in fast recovery signals the lost segment has been delivered. TCP Reno sets cwnd = ssthresh and exits fast recovery into congestion avoidance (linear increase).

q: TCP Tahoe differs from TCP Reno on triple duplicate ACKs by:
options: Always resetting cwnd to 1 MSS (same as timeout behaviour)
explanation: TCP Tahoe treats triple dup-ACKs the same as a timeout: ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = 1 MSS, and it restarts slow start. TCP Reno distinguishes the two events and uses fast recovery.

q: What happens to ssthresh when TCP detects a timeout?
options: ssthresh is not changed on timeout
explanation: On a timeout, TCP halves ssthresh (ssthresh = max(cwnd/2, 2Ã—MSS)) and resets cwnd to 1 MSS. This records the estimated safe operating point at half the current window.

q: Starting from cwnd = 1 MSS and ssthresh = 8 MSS, after 3 RTTs of slow start, cwnd equals:
explanation: Slow start doubles cwnd each RTT: RTT1â†’2, RTT2â†’4, RTT3â†’8. cwnd reaches ssthresh (8 MSS) after 3 RTTs, triggering a switch to congestion avoidance.

q: Which statement about TCP's congestion window (cwnd) evolution is correct?
options: cwnd is advertised by the receiver
cwnd is maintained by the sender and limits data in flight based on network conditions
cwnd is fixed at connection setup
cwnd equals the MSS throughout the connection
explanation: cwnd is a sender-side variable that estimates how much data the network can handle. It is not communicated to the receiver; it is updated by the sender based on ACKs and loss events.

q: Which TCP mechanism allows a receiver to acknowledge out-of-order segments selectively?
explanation: SACK (TCP option) lets the receiver report non-contiguous blocks of received data. This allows the sender to retransmit only the specific missing segments rather than everything from the last ACK.

q: TCP provides reliable delivery through all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Guaranteed delivery within a fixed time bound
explanation: TCP guarantees eventual reliable delivery but not within any fixed time. It uses sequence numbers, ACKs, and retransmission for reliability, and congestion/flow control for efficiencyâ€”but has no timing guarantees.

q: Which comparison between TCP and UDP is CORRECT?
options: UDP provides flow control; TCP does not
TCP is connection-oriented; UDP is connectionless
UDP guarantees in-order delivery; TCP does not
TCP header is smaller than UDP header
explanation: TCP is connection-oriented (three-way handshake required); UDP is connectionless. TCP provides flow/congestion control and reliable in-order delivery. UDP header (8B) is smaller than TCP header (20B minimum).

q: An application that requires low overhead, supports multicast, and can tolerate packet loss should use:
explanation: UDP is lightweight (8-byte header, no connection setup), natively supports multicast/broadcast, and does not retransmit lost packetsâ€”ideal for multicast video or telemetry that tolerates loss.

q: The PSH flag in TCP instructs the receiver to:
options: Immediately push buffered data to the application
explanation: The PSH (Push) flag tells the TCP receiver not to wait for its buffer to fill but to immediately deliver the data to the application layer. It is commonly set for interactive applications.

q: The RST flag in a TCP segment is used to:
options: Abruptly reset/abort the connection due to error or invalid state
explanation: RST (Reset) abruptly terminates the connection. It is sent when a segment arrives for a non-existent connection, or when an application wants to abort rather than gracefully close.

q: TCP's URG flag and Urgent Pointer are used to:
options: Mark data as out-of-band/urgent so the receiver processes it ahead of regular data
explanation: The URG flag marks urgent data; the Urgent Pointer field gives the offset to the last byte of urgent data. The receiver is signalled to process this data before the rest of the buffer.

q: In TCP, which field is used to detect transmission errors in the header and data?
explanation: The TCP Checksum covers the TCP header, data, and a pseudo-header (source IP, dest IP, protocol, TCP length). It detects bit errors during transmission and is mandatory in TCP.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between TCP's rwnd and cwnd?
options: rwnd controls network congestion; cwnd controls receiver buffer
rwnd is the receiver's advertised window; cwnd is the sender's congestion estimate; sender uses min(rwnd, cwnd)
cwnd is sent in the TCP header; rwnd is maintained internally
explanation: rwnd (receiver window) is advertised by the receiver in the Window Size field to signal buffer space. cwnd (congestion window) is maintained by the sender to estimate network capacity. The sender uses min(cwnd, rwnd) as the effective window.

q: Which protocol uses TCP instead of UDP and why?
options: DNS â€” because DNS needs reliability for all queries
HTTP â€” because web content requires reliable, ordered delivery
VoIP â€” because voice needs low latency
Video streaming â€” because video tolerates packet loss
explanation: HTTP uses TCP because web page content (HTML, images, scripts) must be delivered completely and in order. A missing byte would corrupt the page, making reliability essential. VoIP and live streaming prefer UDP for low latency.

q: What is the purpose of the Initial Sequence Number (ISN) being randomly chosen in TCP?
options: To prevent old duplicate segments from a previous connection being accepted by a new connection
explanation: A random ISN ensures that segments from an old (recently closed) connection with the same port pair cannot be mistaken for valid data in a new connection. This prevents TCP sequence number attacks and stale segment confusion.

q: During TCP congestion avoidance, if cwnd = 10 MSS and one RTT elapses with all ACKs received, cwnd becomes approximately:
explanation: In congestion avoidance, cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT (additive increase). So cwnd goes from 10 MSS to 11 MSS after one RTT of successful transmission.

q: Which port does DNS use by default?
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) uses port 53 for both UDP (for standard queries) and TCP (for zone transfers and large responses).

q: What is the correct order of DNS hierarchy from top to bottom?
options: Root â†’ TLD â†’ Second-level â†’ Subdomain
TLD â†’ Root â†’ Subdomain â†’ Second-level
Root â†’ Second-level â†’ TLD â†’ Subdomain
Subdomain â†’ Second-level â†’ TLD â†’ Root
explanation: The DNS hierarchy goes: Root (.) at the top, then TLDs (.com, .org), then second-level domains (example.com), then subdomains (www.example.com).

q: Which DNS record type maps a domain name to an IPv4 address?
explanation: An 'A' record (Address record) maps a hostname to a 32-bit IPv4 address. AAAA records map to 128-bit IPv6 addresses.

q: Which DNS record type maps a domain name to an IPv6 address?
explanation: An 'AAAA' record maps a hostname to a 128-bit IPv6 address. It is called quad-A because IPv6 is four times the size of IPv4.

q: Which DNS record type is used to specify the mail server responsible for accepting email for a domain?
explanation: MX (Mail Exchanger) records specify the mail server(s) responsible for receiving email on behalf of a domain. They include a priority value.

q: A CNAME record in DNS is used to:
options: Create an alias from one domain name to another
explanation: A CNAME (Canonical Name) record creates an alias pointing one domain name to another (canonical) domain name, not directly to an IP address.

q: Which DNS record type is used for reverse DNS lookup (IP to domain name)?
explanation: PTR (Pointer) records are used for reverse DNS lookups â€” mapping an IP address back to a hostname. They are stored in the in-addr.arpa zone.

q: Which DNS record type identifies the authoritative name server for a domain?
explanation: NS (Name Server) records specify the authoritative DNS servers for a domain. These servers hold the actual DNS records for that domain.

q: In iterative DNS resolution, the DNS resolver:
options: Contacts each DNS server itself based on referrals
Asks one server to fully resolve the query
Uses a cached result without querying
Broadcasts the query to all servers
explanation: In iterative resolution, the client's resolver contacts each DNS server in turn. Each server either answers or refers the resolver to the next server. The resolver does the work.

q: In recursive DNS resolution, the DNS server:
options: Returns a referral to the next server
Fully resolves the query on behalf of the client
Drops the query if not in cache
Sends a broadcast to all name servers
explanation: In recursive resolution, the DNS server takes full responsibility for resolving the query. It contacts other DNS servers as needed and returns the final answer to the client.

q: Which protocol and port does SMTP use?
explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) uses TCP port 25 for sending and relaying email between mail servers. It is a push protocol.

q: POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) operates on which port?
explanation: POP3 uses TCP port 110. It downloads emails from the server to the client and typically deletes them from the server after download.

q: IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) uses which port?
explanation: IMAP uses TCP port 143. Unlike POP3, IMAP keeps emails on the server and allows synchronization across multiple devices.

q: What is the primary difference between POP3 and IMAP?
options: POP3 uses UDP; IMAP uses TCP
POP3 downloads and deletes mail from server; IMAP keeps mail on server
POP3 is for sending; IMAP is for receiving
POP3 uses port 143; IMAP uses port 110
explanation: POP3 downloads emails to the client and deletes them from the server, making them inaccessible from other devices. IMAP keeps emails on the server, enabling multi-device access.

q: MIME in email stands for:
explanation: MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) extends the format of email to support text in character sets other than ASCII, attachments, and multimedia content.

q: Which MIME content-type header is used for plain text email?
explanation: The MIME content-type 'text/plain' is used for unformatted plain text. 'text/html' is used for HTML-formatted email content.

q: HTTP is described as a stateless protocol because:
options: It does not use TCP connections
Each request-response pair is independent; the server retains no session state
It uses UDP which is connectionless
explanation: HTTP is stateless because each HTTP request is independent. The server does not retain any information about previous requests. Cookies and sessions are used to simulate state.

q: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve a resource from the server?
explanation: The GET method requests a representation of a specified resource. It should only retrieve data and should have no other effect on the server.

q: Which HTTP method is used to submit data to be processed by the server?
explanation: The POST method submits data to the server (e.g., form data), often resulting in a state change or side effects. Unlike GET, it includes a request body.

q: Which HTTP method is used to update or replace a resource at a specific URL?
explanation: The PUT method replaces all current representations of the target resource with the request payload. It is idempotent â€” repeated calls produce the same result.

q: Which HTTP method is used to remove a resource from the server?
explanation: The DELETE method removes the specified resource from the server. It is idempotent â€” deleting an already-deleted resource still returns success.

q: HTTP status code 200 means:
explanation: HTTP 200 OK indicates the request has succeeded. The response body contains the requested resource or result.

q: HTTP status code 404 indicates:
explanation: HTTP 404 Not Found means the server cannot find the requested resource. The URL may be wrong or the resource may have been deleted.

q: HTTP status code 301 represents:
explanation: HTTP 301 Moved Permanently indicates that the resource has been permanently moved to a new URL. Browsers and search engines update their links accordingly.

q: Which range of HTTP status codes represents server-side errors?
explanation: 5xx status codes (500â€“599) indicate server-side errors where the server failed to fulfill a valid request. Examples: 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable.

q: HTTP status codes in the 4xx range indicate:
explanation: 4xx status codes indicate client-side errors â€” the request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled. Examples: 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found.

q: In HTTP/1.1, persistent connections mean:
options: A new TCP connection is opened for each request
Multiple requests can be sent over a single TCP connection
The server keeps data in memory permanently
UDP is used instead of TCP
explanation: HTTP/1.1 uses persistent connections (keep-alive) by default, allowing multiple HTTP request/response pairs over a single TCP connection, reducing latency and overhead.

q: Non-persistent HTTP connections differ from persistent connections in that:
options: Each object requires a separate TCP connection
Multiple objects share one TCP connection
Non-persistent is used only in HTTP/2
explanation: In non-persistent HTTP (default in HTTP/1.0), a new TCP connection is established for each object. This causes higher latency due to repeated TCP handshakes.

q: HTTPS uses which default port?
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443. It is HTTP layered over TLS/SSL, providing encryption, authentication, and data integrity.

q: HTTP operates on which default port?
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) operates on port 80 by default. HTTPS operates on port 443.

q: HTTP 1xx status codes represent:
explanation: 1xx status codes are informational â€” they indicate that the request was received and the process is continuing. Example: 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols.

q: Which port does FTP use for control connections?
explanation: FTP uses port 21 for control connections (commands and responses between client and server). Port 20 is used for data transfer in active mode.

q: Which port does FTP use for data transfer in active mode?
explanation: In FTP active mode, the server initiates the data connection from its port 20 to a client-specified port. The control connection always uses port 21.

q: In FTP active mode:
options: Client initiates both control and data connections
Server initiates the data connection to the client
Client uses a fixed port for data
No separate data channel is used
explanation: In active FTP, the client opens a control connection to server port 21, then the server initiates a data connection from its port 20 to the client's specified data port.

q: In FTP passive mode:
options: Client initiates both control and data connections
Port 20 is always used for data
FTP uses UDP for data transfer
explanation: In passive FTP, the client initiates both control and data connections. The server opens a random port and tells the client, which then connects to it. This helps with firewalls/NAT.

q: Why is FTP passive mode preferred over active mode in modern networks?
options: Passive mode avoids firewall issues by having the client initiate data connections
explanation: In active mode, the server initiates the data connection, which is often blocked by client-side firewalls/NAT. Passive mode has the client initiate the data connection, making it firewall-friendly.

q: FTP transmits data including credentials in:
explanation: Standard FTP transmits all data including usernames and passwords in plain text, making it insecure. FTPS (FTP Secure) and SFTP provide encrypted alternatives.

q: Telnet operates on which port?
explanation: Telnet uses TCP port 23. It provides a bidirectional interactive text-oriented communication facility but transmits all data including passwords in plain text.

q: SSH (Secure Shell) operates on which port?
explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) uses TCP port 22. It provides encrypted remote login, command execution, and other network services over an unsecured network.

q: What is the main advantage of SSH over Telnet?
options: SSH encrypts all communication; Telnet sends data in plain text
SSH uses UDP; Telnet uses TCP
SSH supports more commands than Telnet
explanation: SSH encrypts all traffic including passwords, preventing eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. Telnet transmits everything in plain text, making it a security risk.

q: Which protocol replaced Telnet as the standard for secure remote access?
explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) replaced Telnet because it provides the same remote terminal functionality with encryption, authentication, and data integrity features.

q: SNMP stands for:
explanation: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is an application-layer protocol used for monitoring and managing network devices like routers, switches, servers, and printers.

q: SNMP uses which transport protocol and port?
explanation: SNMP uses UDP port 161 for queries from the manager to agents, and UDP port 162 for SNMP traps (unsolicited alerts from agents to the manager).

q: In SNMP architecture, the component that monitors network devices is called:
explanation: The SNMP Manager (NMS - Network Management Station) monitors and controls managed devices. The SNMP Agent runs on managed devices and reports to the manager.

q: MIB in SNMP stands for:
explanation: MIB (Management Information Base) is a hierarchical database of information about network devices managed by SNMP. Each variable in the MIB is identified by an OID (Object Identifier).

q: Which SNMP operation allows the manager to retrieve a value from an agent?
explanation: The SNMP GET operation allows the manager to request the value of one or more variables from an agent. The agent responds with the requested values.

q: An SNMP TRAP is:
options: A request from manager to agent
An unsolicited alert sent by an agent to the manager
An error message from the manager
explanation: An SNMP TRAP is an asynchronous notification sent by an agent to the manager without being polled, alerting the manager to significant events like link failures or threshold breaches.

q: Which HTTP status code indicates 'Not Modified' (used with caching)?
explanation: HTTP 304 Not Modified tells the client that the cached version of the requested resource is still valid, so the server does not return the resource again, saving bandwidth.

q: The DNS root zone contains how many root name servers (groups)?
explanation: There are 13 logical root name server addresses (labeled A through M), operated by various organizations. Physically, hundreds of servers are distributed worldwide using anycast routing.

q: Which of the following correctly pairs the protocol with its default port?
explanation: The standard port assignments are: SMTP (sending mail) â€” port 25, POP3 (downloading mail) â€” port 110, IMAP (mail synchronization) â€” port 143.

q: In DNS, a zone file for 'example.com' contains 'www IN CNAME example.com.' This means:
options: www is an IPv4 address record
www is an alias pointing to example.com
example.com is an alias for www
www is the authoritative name server
explanation: A CNAME record creates an alias. 'www IN CNAME example.com' means www.example.com is an alias (canonical name) pointing to example.com. DNS resolves example.com to get the actual IP.

q: Which security property ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to access it?
explanation: Confidentiality ensures that data is accessible only to authorized parties. It is typically achieved through encryption. Integrity ensures data is not altered; Authentication verifies identity; Non-repudiation prevents denial of actions.

q: Which security property guarantees that data has not been altered during transmission?
explanation: Integrity ensures that data remains unaltered during transmission or storage. Hash functions (MD5, SHA) and MACs are used to provide integrity. If data is tampered, the hash value changes, alerting the receiver.

q: Which security property verifies that the communicating entity is who it claims to be?
explanation: Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system. It can be based on something you know (password), something you have (token), or something you are (biometrics). Digital certificates also provide authentication.

q: Which security property prevents a sender from denying that they sent a message?
explanation: Non-repudiation ensures that a party cannot deny having sent or received a message. It is achieved through digital signatures â€” only the sender's private key can produce the signature, providing undeniable proof of origin.

q: In symmetric encryption, which of the following is true?
options: Different keys are used for encryption and decryption
The same key is used for both encryption and decryption
A public key encrypts and a private key decrypts
explanation: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for both encryption and decryption. Examples include AES, DES, and 3DES. The main challenge is securely distributing the shared key to all parties.

q: Which of the following is an example of a symmetric encryption algorithm?
explanation: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is a symmetric encryption algorithm widely used today. RSA and ECC are asymmetric algorithms. Diffie-Hellman is a key exchange protocol, not an encryption algorithm itself.

q: In asymmetric encryption, a message encrypted with the recipient's public key can be decrypted by:
explanation: In asymmetric (public-key) encryption, a message encrypted with the recipient's public key can only be decrypted by the recipient's corresponding private key. This ensures confidentiality â€” only the intended recipient can read it.

q: Which of the following is an asymmetric encryption algorithm?
explanation: RSA (Rivestâ€“Shamirâ€“Adleman) is an asymmetric encryption algorithm based on the difficulty of factoring large integers. AES, DES, and Blowfish are all symmetric encryption algorithms.

q: Which encryption type is generally faster and more suitable for encrypting large amounts of data?
explanation: Symmetric encryption is computationally faster than asymmetric encryption and is preferred for bulk data encryption. Asymmetric encryption is slower due to complex mathematical operations; it is typically used only for key exchange or small data.

q: What is the primary disadvantage of symmetric key cryptography?
options: It is too slow for practical use
Secure key distribution between parties is difficult
explanation: The key distribution problem is the main drawback of symmetric cryptography. Both parties must share the same secret key, but securely exchanging it over an insecure network is challenging. Asymmetric cryptography solves this.

q: A digital signature is created by encrypting the message hash with the sender's:
explanation: A digital signature is created by encrypting the hash of the message with the sender's private key. The receiver decrypts it using the sender's public key and compares it with the computed hash. This provides authentication and non-repudiation.

q: To verify a digital signature, the receiver uses the sender's:
explanation: The receiver verifies a digital signature by decrypting it with the sender's public key to get the hash, then independently computes the hash of the received message. If they match, the signature is valid, confirming authenticity and integrity.

q: Which of the following properties does a digital signature provide? (Choose the best answer)
explanation: Digital signatures provide Authentication (who sent it), Integrity (message not altered), and Non-repudiation (sender cannot deny it). They do NOT provide confidentiality â€” for that, the message itself must be encrypted.

q: A digital certificate binds a public key to:
explanation: A digital certificate binds a public key to an identity (person, server, or organization). It is issued and digitally signed by a Certificate Authority (CA), confirming that the public key truly belongs to the stated identity.

q: Who issues digital certificates to verify the authenticity of public keys?
explanation: A Certificate Authority (CA) is a trusted third party that issues digital certificates. Examples include DigiCert, Verisign, and Let's Encrypt. The CA digitally signs the certificate, vouching for the binding of the public key to the identity.

q: Which standard defines the format of digital certificates?
explanation: X.509 is the ITU-T standard that defines the format for public key certificates (digital certificates). It is the most widely used certificate format, employed in SSL/TLS, S/MIME, and many other protocols.

q: A firewall primarily operates by:
options: Monitoring and filtering network traffic based on rules
Authenticating users before granting network access
explanation: A firewall monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules (ACLs). It acts as a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks like the Internet.

q: A packet-filtering firewall makes decisions based on:
options: Source/destination IP, port numbers, and protocol
explanation: A packet-filtering firewall examines packet headers and filters based on source/destination IP addresses, port numbers, and transport protocol (TCP/UDP). It does not inspect application-layer content or maintain session state.

q: Which type of firewall maintains state information about active network connections?
explanation: A stateful inspection firewall (also called a dynamic packet filter) tracks the state of active connections in a state table. It can distinguish valid packets belonging to an established connection from unsolicited packets, providing better security.

q: An application-layer firewall (proxy firewall) works at which OSI layer?
explanation: An application-layer firewall (proxy firewall) operates at Layer 7 (Application layer) of the OSI model. It can inspect the actual content of application-layer protocols (HTTP, FTP, DNS) and filter based on content, not just headers.

q: IPv6 uses how many bits for its address space?
explanation: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses, providing 2^128 (approximately 3.4 Ã— 10^38) unique addresses. This is a massive expansion from IPv4's 32-bit addresses (about 4.3 billion), designed to solve IPv4 address exhaustion.

q: An IPv6 address is represented in which notation?
options: Eight groups of four hexadecimal digits separated by colons
explanation: IPv6 addresses are written as eight groups of four hexadecimal digits, separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334). Consecutive groups of zeros can be replaced with :: once in an address.

q: How many bits are in one group (hextet) of an IPv6 address?
explanation: An IPv6 address has 8 groups (hextets), each consisting of 4 hexadecimal digits = 16 bits. Total = 8 Ã— 16 = 128 bits. Each hexadecimal digit represents 4 bits, so 4 hex digits = 4 Ã— 4 = 16 bits.

q: The double-colon (::) in an IPv6 address represents:
options: One or more consecutive groups of all zeros
The separation between network and host portions
explanation: The double-colon (::) is a shorthand notation in IPv6 that replaces one or more consecutive groups of all-zero hextets. For example, 2001:db8::1 expands to 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. It can only appear once in an address.

q: Compared to IPv4, the IPv6 header is described as:
options: More complex with many optional fields
Simplified with a fixed size and fewer fields
Identical in structure but with larger addresses
Variable in length with more options
explanation: The IPv6 header is simplified â€” it has a fixed size of 40 bytes and only 8 fields, compared to IPv4's variable-length header with 12+ fields. Rarely-used IPv4 fields (like checksum, fragmentation) were removed or moved to extension headers.

q: Which IPv4 header field was removed in IPv6 to simplify the header?
explanation: The Header Checksum field was removed from IPv6 to simplify processing. Since error detection is handled by Layer 2 (Ethernet) and Layer 4 (TCP/UDP), the IPv6 designers eliminated this redundant field to speed up router processing.

q: In IPv6, which field replaces the IPv4 'Time to Live (TTL)' field?
explanation: IPv6 uses the Hop Limit field instead of IPv4's TTL (Time to Live). Both serve the same purpose â€” decremented by 1 at each router, and the packet is discarded when it reaches 0, preventing infinite loops.

q: The 'Next Header' field in IPv6 serves a similar purpose to which IPv4 field?
explanation: The IPv6 'Next Header' field identifies the type of the next header following the IPv6 header â€” similar to IPv4's 'Protocol' field. It can indicate transport protocol (TCP=6, UDP=17) or the type of an IPv6 extension header.

q: What is the IPv6 dual-stack technique?
options: Running two IPv6 addresses on one interface
A device running both IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously
Using two separate routers for IPv4 and IPv6
explanation: Dual-stack means a device (host or router) runs both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks simultaneously. It can communicate with both IPv4-only and IPv6-only devices. This is the most common IPv4-to-IPv6 transition mechanism.

q: In IPv6 tunneling, IPv6 packets are:
options: Encapsulated inside IPv4 packets to cross IPv4 networks
Sent over dedicated IPv6 links only
explanation: In IPv6 tunneling, IPv6 packets are encapsulated (wrapped) inside IPv4 packets to traverse IPv4-only networks. At the tunnel endpoints, the IPv4 wrapper is added (encapsulation) and removed (decapsulation). 6to4 and ISATAP are common tunneling mechanisms.

q: Which IPv6 transition mechanism translates IPv6 addresses to IPv4 addresses and vice versa?
explanation: NAT64 (with DNS64) is a translation mechanism that allows IPv6-only devices to communicate with IPv4-only devices by translating IPv6 addresses to IPv4 addresses and vice versa. It is different from tunneling (which encapsulates without translating).

q: A socket in networking is defined as:
options: A physical network port on a switch
The combination of an IP address and a port number
A type of network cable connector
explanation: A socket is defined as the combination of an IP address and a port number (e.g., 192.168.1.5:80). It uniquely identifies a specific process/service on a specific host. A network connection is identified by a pair of sockets (source socket + destination socket).

q: What is the range of well-known port numbers?
explanation: Well-known ports range from 0 to 1023. They are assigned by IANA to common, standardized services: HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), FTP (21), SSH (22), DNS (53), SMTP (25). These require root/administrator privileges to bind.

q: Which port number is assigned to HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)?
explanation: HTTP uses well-known port 80. HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443. FTP uses port 21. SMTP uses port 25. These are standardized well-known ports in the range 0â€“1023, assigned by IANA.

q: What is the range of registered (user) port numbers?
explanation: Registered ports range from 1024 to 49151. They are registered with IANA for specific applications but can be used without administrator privileges. Examples: MySQL (3306), RDP (3389), PostgreSQL (5432).

q: Ephemeral (dynamic) ports are used for:
options: Well-known server services like HTTP and FTP
Short-lived client-side ports assigned temporarily for outgoing connections
explanation: Ephemeral ports (range 49152â€“65535) are temporary ports assigned by the OS to client-side sockets for outgoing connections. They are released when the connection closes. For example, when a browser connects to a web server, the OS assigns an ephemeral port to the browser.

q: What is the range of ephemeral (dynamic/private) port numbers as defined by IANA?
explanation: IANA defines ephemeral (dynamic/private) ports as 49152 to 65535. These are used for temporary client connections. Note: Linux by default uses 32768â€“60999, but the IANA-defined ephemeral range is 49152â€“65535.

q: Port 443 is associated with which protocol?
explanation: Port 443 is the well-known port for HTTPS (HTTP Secure), which is HTTP over TLS/SSL. It provides encrypted and authenticated communication between web browsers and servers. Port 80 is plain HTTP, port 22 is SSH, port 23 is Telnet.

q: Which port number is used by DNS (Domain Name System)?
explanation: DNS uses port 53 (both TCP and UDP). It is used for domain name resolution â€” converting hostnames to IP addresses. UDP port 53 is used for standard queries; TCP port 53 is used for zone transfers and large responses.

q: Which layer of the OSI model do sockets primarily operate at?
explanation: Sockets operate at Layer 4 (Transport layer) of the OSI model, combining the IP address (Layer 3) and port number (Layer 4) to uniquely identify a communication endpoint. The Socket API provides applications access to transport-layer services.

q: Which security goal is directly addressed by using a Message Authentication Code (MAC)?
explanation: A MAC (Message Authentication Code) provides both integrity (detects alteration) and authentication (confirms the sender knows the shared secret key). However, it does NOT provide non-repudiation because the secret key is shared â€” either party could have created it.

q: In the context of PKI (Public Key Infrastructure), the chain of trust refers to:
options: A sequence of encryption keys used in a session
The hierarchical chain from root CA through intermediate CAs to end-entity certificates
A linked list of encrypted messages
The sequence of hops a packet takes through the network
explanation: The chain of trust in PKI is the hierarchical structure from the Root CA (self-signed, inherently trusted) through Intermediate CAs down to end-entity (leaf) certificates. Each certificate is signed by its parent CA, allowing verification up to a trusted root.

q: Which of the following best describes a man-in-the-middle attack that digital certificates help prevent?
options: An attacker flooding the network with packets
An attacker intercepting and impersonating communicating parties
An attacker guessing passwords through brute force
An attacker exploiting buffer overflow vulnerabilities
explanation: A man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack occurs when an attacker secretly intercepts and possibly alters communication between two parties. Digital certificates prevent this by binding a public key to a verified identity â€” a client can confirm the server's authenticity before sharing data.

q: Which IPv6 address type is equivalent to IPv4's broadcast address?
options: IPv6 has no broadcast; it uses multicast instead
explanation: IPv6 does not support broadcast addresses. Instead, it uses multicast to send packets to multiple recipients simultaneously. For example, the all-nodes multicast address (ff02::1) replaces IPv4's subnet broadcast. This reduces network congestion.

q: The IPv6 loopback address is:
explanation: The IPv6 loopback address is ::1 (expanded: 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001), equivalent to IPv4's 127.0.0.1. It is used to test the local network stack without sending traffic on the network.

q: Which type of IPv6 address starts with FE80::/10 and is used for communication within a single network segment?
explanation: Link-local addresses (FE80::/10) are automatically configured on every IPv6-enabled interface and are valid only within a single network segment (link). They are used for neighbor discovery, router discovery, and cannot be routed across the Internet.

q: Which firewall technique inspects traffic at the application layer and can understand specific protocols like HTTP?
explanation: Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) / Application-layer gateways (proxy firewalls) inspect the content of packets at the application layer. They understand specific protocols (HTTP, FTP, DNS) and can filter based on application data, not just headers.

q: What is the purpose of a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
options: To completely block all external access to the internal network
To create an isolated network segment for publicly accessible servers, protected from the internal network
To encrypt all traffic between the internal and external networks
To monitor traffic between two internal network segments
explanation: A DMZ is a perimeter network that sits between the internal network and the Internet. Public-facing servers (web, email, DNS) are placed in the DMZ. Even if they are compromised, the internal network remains protected by the inner firewall.

q: How many total IP addresses can IPv6 support?
explanation: IPv6 supports 2^128 â‰ˆ 3.4 Ã— 10^38 addresses (about 340 undecillion). This is astronomically larger than IPv4's 2^32 â‰ˆ 4.3 billion addresses, effectively solving IPv4 address exhaustion for the foreseeable future.

q: In a TCP connection, which socket pair uniquely identifies the connection?
explanation: A TCP connection is uniquely identified by a 4-tuple: (Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port). This allows a single server (e.g., web server on port 80) to maintain thousands of simultaneous connections from different clients.

q: SSH (Secure Shell) uses which well-known port number?
explanation: SSH uses well-known port 22. SSH provides encrypted remote login and command execution, replacing the insecure Telnet (port 23). Port 20 is FTP data transfer, port 21 is FTP control connection.

q: Which of the following correctly pairs a protocol with its well-known port?
explanation: FTP Control uses port 21 (FTP Data uses port 20). SMTP (email sending) uses port 25, not 110. POP3 (email retrieval) uses port 110, not 25. HTTPS uses port 443, not 80 (that is plain HTTP).

q: A host is assigned the IP address 192.168.10.0/26. How many usable host addresses are available in this subnet?
explanation: /26 means 6 host bits. Total addresses = 2^6 = 64. Usable = 64 - 2 (network + broadcast) = 62.

q: How many subnets are created when a Class C network 192.168.1.0 is subnetted with mask /28?
explanation: Class C has 24 network bits. /28 borrows 4 bits for subnetting. 2^4 = 16 subnets.

q: The CIDR block 10.0.0.0/22 contains how many IP addresses?
explanation: /22 leaves 32-22 = 10 host bits. 2^10 = 1024 addresses total.

q: Which is the broadcast address of the subnet 172.16.5.64/26?
explanation: /26 block size = 64. Subnet starts at .64, so broadcast = .64 + 63 = .127.

q: A datagram of 4000 bytes (including 20-byte IP header) is fragmented over a link with MTU = 1500 bytes. What is the fragment offset (in 8-byte units) of the second fragment?
explanation: Each fragment payload = 1500 - 20 = 1480 bytes. First fragment carries bytes 0â€“1479. Second fragment offset = 1480/8 = 185.

q: A 5000-byte IP datagram (20-byte header) is fragmented with MTU = 1500 bytes. How many fragments are created?
explanation: Data payload = 5000 - 20 = 4980 bytes. Each fragment payload = 1480 bytes. Fragments = ceil(4980/1480) = ceil(3.36) = 4.

q: In IP fragmentation, what is the fragment offset (in 8-byte units) of the second fragment of a 3000-byte datagram (20-byte header) fragmented with MTU = 1500?
explanation: Fragment payload = 1500-20 = 1480 bytes. First fragment: bytes 0â€“1479. Second fragment offset = 1480/8 = 185 units.

q: For Go-Back-N with window size W=4 and a = Tp/Tf = 10, the link efficiency is:
explanation: GBN efficiency = W/(1+2a) when W < 1+2a. Here 1+2(10) = 21 and W=4 < 21, so Î· = 4/21 â‰ˆ 0.19.

q: For Selective Repeat with window size W=16 and a=5, the efficiency is:
explanation: SR efficiency = W/(1+2a) if W < 1+2a, else 1. Here 1+2(5) = 11 and W=16 > 11, so efficiency = 1 (100%).

q: For Go-Back-N with a 3-bit sequence number field, what is the maximum sender window size?
explanation: 3-bit sequence number gives 2^3 = 8 sequence numbers. GBN max window = 2^n - 1 = 7.

q: In Stop-and-Wait ARQ, propagation delay Tp = 2 ms and frame transmission time Tf = 1 ms. What is the link utilization?
In pure ALOHA, what is the maximum channel throughput?
explanation: Pure ALOHA maximum throughput = 1/(2e) â‰ˆ 0.184 = 18.4% at offered load G = 0.5.

q: Slotted ALOHA achieves a maximum throughput of:
explanation: Slotted ALOHA max throughput = 1/e â‰ˆ 0.368 = 36.8% at G=1. This is exactly twice pure ALOHA.

q: TCP slow start begins with cwnd = 1 MSS. After 3 RTTs with no congestion, the congestion window is:
explanation: Slow start doubles cwnd each RTT: start=1, RTT1â†’2, RTT2â†’4, RTT3â†’8 MSS.

q: TCP is in congestion avoidance with cwnd = 16 MSS. A timeout occurs. The new ssthresh and cwnd are:
explanation: On timeout: ssthresh = cwnd/2 = 8 MSS, cwnd = 1 MSS. TCP re-enters slow start.

q: TCP receives 3 duplicate ACKs when cwnd = 12 MSS. After fast retransmit (Reno), ssthresh and cwnd become:
explanation: On 3 dup-ACKs (TCP Reno): ssthresh = cwnd/2 = 6, cwnd = ssthresh = 6. TCP enters congestion avoidance.

q: With ssthresh = 8 MSS and initial cwnd = 1 MSS, after how many RTTs does TCP switch from slow start to congestion avoidance?
explanation: Slow start doubles each RTT: 1â†’2â†’4â†’8. After 3 RTTs, cwnd = 8 = ssthresh. TCP switches at the beginning of RTT 4.

q: The CRC remainder of message 1101 divided by generator 1011 (degree 3) is:
explanation: Append 3 zeros: 1101000. XOR with 1011: 1101000 XOR 1011000 = 0110000; 110 XOR 011=... Performing binary division: 1101000 Ã· 1011 â†’ quotient steps yield remainder 001. Transmitted frame = 1101001.

q: For Hamming code with 4 data bits, how many parity bits are required?
explanation: Hamming condition: 2^r â‰¥ r + d + 1. For d=4: 2^3=8 â‰¥ 3+4+1=8. So r=3 parity bits are needed.

q: A Hamming code with r=4 parity bits can correct single-bit errors in a codeword of maximum length:
explanation: With r=4: 2^4 = 16. Maximum codeword length = 2^r - 1 = 15 bits (4 parity + 11 data bits).

q: A link has bandwidth = 10 Mbps and propagation delay = 20 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is:
explanation: BDP = bandwidth Ã— propagation delay = 10Ã—10^6 Ã— 20Ã—10^-3 = 200,000 bits.

q: A network link has bandwidth = 1 Gbps and RTT = 40 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is:
explanation: BDP = 10^9 Ã— 40Ã—10^-3 = 4Ã—10^7 bits = 40 Mb. This is the 'pipe' capacity in flight.

q: The propagation delay on a 500 km fiber link (speed = 2Ã—10^8 m/s) is:
explanation: Propagation delay = distance/speed = 500,000 / (2Ã—10^8) = 2.5Ã—10^-3 s = 2.5 ms.

q: If a 1500-byte frame is sent over a 100 Mbps link, the transmission delay is:
The total delay for a 1000-byte packet on a 10 Mbps link with propagation delay 5 ms is:
explanation: Transmission delay = (1000Ã—8)/(10Ã—10^6) = 0.8 ms. Total = 0.8 + 5 = 5.8 ms.

q: Well-known port number for HTTP is:
explanation: HTTP uses TCP port 80. HTTPS=443, FTP control=21, SMTP=25.

q: Which port number does DNS use?
explanation: DNS uses port 53 (UDP and TCP). DHCP uses 67/68, TFTP uses 69, NTP uses 123.

q: SMTP operates on which TCP port?
explanation: SMTP uses TCP port 25 for mail transfer. POP3=110, IMAP=143, SMTPS=465.

q: At which OSI layer does IP operate?
explanation: IP is a Layer 3 (Network layer) protocol responsible for logical addressing and routing.

q: TCP and UDP operate at which OSI layer?
explanation: TCP and UDP are Transport layer (Layer 4) protocols providing end-to-end communication services.

q: ICMP operates at which OSI layer?
explanation: ICMP is encapsulated directly in IP and operates at Layer 3 (Network layer). Used for error reporting (ping, traceroute).

q: A subnet has address 10.1.2.128/25. What is the valid host address range?
explanation: /25 block size = 128. Network = 10.1.2.128, Broadcast = 10.1.2.255. Valid hosts = .129 to .254.

q: How many usable host addresses does a /30 subnet provide?
explanation: /30 has 2 host bits. Total = 4 addresses. Usable = 4 - 2 = 2 host addresses. Used for point-to-point links.

q: The number of usable hosts in a /22 subnet is:
explanation: /22 has 32-22=10 host bits. Total = 1024 addresses. Usable = 1024 - 2 = 1022 hosts.

q: The minimum sequence number bits required for Selective Repeat with window size W = 32 is:
explanation: SR requires sequence space â‰¥ 2W = 64. Bits needed = log2(64) = 6.

q: A sender transmits at 1 Mbps, frame size = 1000 bits, Tp = 4 ms. Minimum GBN window size for 100% efficiency is:
explanation: Tf = 1000/10^6 = 1 ms. a = Tp/Tf = 4. 1+2a = 9. Need W â‰¥ 9 for full GBN efficiency.

q: If pure ALOHA has offered load G = 1, the throughput S is approximately:
explanation: Pure ALOHA: S = GÂ·e^(-2G) = 1Â·e^(-2) â‰ˆ 0.135. Maximum S=0.184 occurs at G=0.5.

q: In CRC, the number of bits appended to the message equals:
options: The degree of the generator polynomial
The number of 1-bits in the generator
explanation: CRC appends r bits where r = degree of the generator polynomial. The divisor has r+1 bits.

q: In Stop-and-Wait, bandwidth = 10 Mbps, frame = 10,000 bits, propagation delay Tp = 20 ms. The efficiency is:
TCP congestion window starts at 1 MSS, ssthresh = 16 MSS. After 4 RTTs of slow start (no loss), cwnd is:
explanation: Slow start doubles each RTT: 1â†’2â†’4â†’8â†’16. At RTT4, cwnd = 16 = ssthresh. TCP switches to congestion avoidance.

q: Which port does FTP data transfer use?
explanation: FTP uses port 21 for control and port 20 for active data transfer. SSH=22, Telnet=23.

q: HTTPS uses TCP port:
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over TLS) uses TCP port 443. Standard HTTP uses port 80.

q: Class B IP address range spans:
explanation: Class A: 0â€“127, Class B: 128â€“191, Class C: 192â€“223, Class D (multicast): 224â€“239.

q: A 10,000-byte IP datagram (20-byte header) is fragmented with MTU = 3500 bytes. The offset of the second fragment (in 8-byte units) is:
explanation: Fragment payload = 3500-20 = 3480 bytes. First fragment: bytes 0â€“3479. Second fragment offset = 3480/8 = 435 units.

q: In Hamming code, if the syndrome = 0 after receiving a codeword, it indicates:
explanation: Syndrome = 0 means all parity checks pass. No single-bit error is detected. (Even-count multi-bit errors can produce syndrome=0 but go undetected.)

q: In a Hamming(7,4) code, parity bit at position 2 covers which bit positions?
explanation: Position 2 (binary 010) covers all positions with bit 1 set in position 1 (0-indexed): positions 2,3,6,7.

q: For Go-Back-N with window W = 7 and a = 3 (Tp/Tf), the efficiency is:
explanation: 1+2a = 1+6 = 7. Since W = 7 â‰¥ 1+2a = 7, GBN efficiency = 1 (100%). The pipe is exactly full.

q: The ARP protocol operates at which layer boundary?
explanation: ARP resolves Layer 3 (IP) addresses to Layer 2 (MAC) addresses. It is encapsulated in Layer 2 frames but serves Layer 3 needs, placing it at the Layer 2/3 boundary.

q: A file of size 1 MB is transmitted over a 1 Mbps link. The transmission time is:
explanation: Transmission time = file size / bandwidth = (1Ã—10^6 Ã— 8 bits) / (1Ã—10^6 bps) = 8 seconds.

q: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) uses which port?
explanation: SNMP agent listens on UDP port 161. SNMP trap messages are sent to port 162. Syslog uses 514, SSH uses 22.
